text: Reducing intrathoracic esophageal anastomotic leaks and strictures with a novel anastomotic technique. Anastomotic complications represent a significant source of morbidity and occasional mortality after esophagectomy. Since two thousand nine we have used a novel side to side staple line on staple line technique for now over three hundred intrathoracic esophagogastric anastomosis. This technique was designed to create a wide diameter esophagogastric anastomosis while preserving stomach conduit blood supply with the aim of minimizing the risk of strictures and leaks. In this video, we describe the technique used in our institution's experience.

The thoracic portion of the Ivor Lewis esophagectomy is shown with a thoracoscope inserted through a chest tube incision. The patient's spine is positioned towards the left of the screen, while the lung is retracted anteriorly towards the right. The gastric conduit is visible in the foreground, with the staple line of the lesser curve facing laterally.

In the abdomen, a uniform diameter gastric conduit is created using a 100mm stapler with a green cartridge, delivering two rows of staples with a 4.8mm height. Additional fires of a 60mm endoscopic tri-stapler complete the conduit construction, which is then delivered into the chest.

The surgeon aligns the mid-left lateral aspect of the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus over the lesser curve staple line of the gastric conduit. Four initial tacking sutures are placed approximately three centimeters apart to begin the esophagogastric anastomosis, ensuring minimal devascularization by mobilizing the esophagus only to the level of the conduit tip.

The surgeon partially transects the esophagus one centimeter distal to the inferior set of tacking sutures. A corresponding two-centimeter gastrotomy is made in the gastric conduit across the lesser curve staple line.

The surgeon places a total of five tacking sutures between the adjacent conduit and esophageal walls. The first suture is positioned in the middle through the lesser curve staple line, with two additional sutures placed on either side.

The surgeon uses an endoscopic stapler with a 45mm green cartridge to perform the stapled portion of the esophagogastric anastomosis. The narrow anvil is positioned within the esophageal lumen, while the large reload jaw is placed within the conduit lumen. The stapler is fired, restapling and cutting through the previous lesser curve staple line. The side-to-side communication is inspected to ensure complete cutting and stapling, followed by sharp completion of the esophageal transection.

A silk suture is placed at the center of the esophageal opening for retraction. The cut end of the esophagus is visibly longer than the gastrotomy. An elliptical section of the conduit is removed over the lesser curve staple line to preserve the collateral blood supply, rather than extending the gastrotomy laterally.

The surgeon closes the open common lumen with two layers of sutures. The inner layer consists of inverted, interrupted 3-0 polyglyctin sutures, tied after placement. The outer layer uses interrupted 3-0 silk sutures with a limbered technique, starting at both corners. A modified horizontal mattress suture is placed at the junction of the gastric conduit staple line.

The surgeon cuts the initial orienting sutures and inspects the completed anastomosis. Soft tissue coverage is achieved using a pleural flap mobilized from the superior aspect over the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus, secured to the conduit with interrupted three-oh silk sutures. In cases with heavily radiated tissues, bulkier coverage using pericardial fat is considered.

The surgeon eliminates any residual redundancy in the conduit by over-sewing the superior aspect of the lesser curve conduit staple line using a limbered technique, extending inferiorly until reaching the right gastric vessels. A nasogastric tube is inserted into the conduit by anesthesia, reaching the level of the cruce. Chest tubes are placed and maintained to water seal.

Esophagectomy plays an important role in the treatment of esophageal cancer and select benign conditions. Gastroesophageal and asthmatic complications, such as leak and stricture, are associated with morbidity, occasional mortality, and increased cost of care. We believe this novel side-to-side, staple-line-on-staple-line technique is easily adapted and provides excellent results with respect to minimizing and asthmatic complications.
